title: Early Judaism and Early Christianity
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on Judaism and early Christianity!

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question. 

>“The Romans have become masters of war . . . when they went in numbers into the lanes of the city, with their swords drawn, they killed those whom they overtook, without mercy, and set fire to the houses from which the Jews had fled, and burnt every soul in them, and laid waste a great many of the rest; and when they came to the houses to plunder them, they found in them entire families of dead men, and the upper rooms full of dead corpses, who died by the famine; they then stood in a horror at this sight, and left without touching anything.”

>From Josephus’ description of the Roman sack of Jerusalem, written c. 75 CE. 

**The events described in this passage most directly contributed to which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growth of Jewish diasporic communities around the Mediterranean
Rome abandoned its attempts to rule over Jewish communities
The relocation of many Jews as part of the Babylonian Exile
The adoption of Christianity as Rome's official religion

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage.

>"By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept

> when we remembered Zion.


> There on the poplars

> we hung our harps,

> for there our captors asked us for songs,

> our tormentors demanded songs of joy;

> they said, “Sing us one of the songs of Zion!”

> How can we sing the songs of the Lord

> while in a foreign land?

> If I forget you, Jerusalem,

> may my right hand forget its skill.

> May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth

> if I do not remember you,

> if I do not consider Jerusalem
    
> my highest joy."

From "The Book of Psalms," 137

**The events described in the passage were the result of which of the following developments in the ancient Middle East?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The sacking of Jerusalem in 70 CE
The formation of Jewish diasporic communities
The development of agriculture near the Tigris and Euphrates rivers
The enslavement of Assyrians in Bablylon

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage.

>"By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept

> when we remembered Zion.


> There on the poplars

> we hung our harps,

> for there our captors asked us for songs,

> our tormentors demanded songs of joy;

> they said, “Sing us one of the songs of Zion!”

> How can we sing the songs of the Lord

> while in a foreign land?

> If I forget you, Jerusalem,

> may my right hand forget its skill.

> May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth

> if I do not remember you,

> if I do not consider Jerusalem
    
> my highest joy."

From "The Book of Psalms," 137

**A historian would most likely use this passage as evidence for which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Codification of a monotheistic religion
Codification of a polytheistic religion
Growing acceptance of Hellenic culture
Growing rejection of Hebrew culture

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question.

>"When I entered Babylon as a friend and when I established the seat of the government in the palace of the ruler under jubilation and rejoicing, Marduk, the great lord, induced the forgiving inhabitants of Babylon to love me, and I was daily trying to worship him . . . I returned to these sacred cities on the other side of the Tigris River the sanctuaries which have been ruins for a long time, the images which used to live in them and established for them permanent sanctuaries. I also gathered all their former inhabitants and returned them to their original homes."

From: Cyrus the Great’s Decree of Return for the Jews, 539 BCE

**The events described in this passage can be best understood in which of the following contexts?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The captivity of Jews in the Babylonian Empire
The conquest of Jerusalem by Cyrus the Great
The abolition of religious freedom in Persia
The conversion of subjects to Persian monotheism

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The image above is most likely the direct result of which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Roman temple of Antoninus and Faustina built in 141 CE, with a Christian cross that was added later.
options.caption: Roman temple of Antoninus and Faustina built in 141 CE, with a Christian cross that was added later. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-images/97bfb92cb18d947e9309a65e05ba92b442932321.jpg)

choices.content: The repurposing of architecture after the spread of Christianity throughout the Roman Empire
The building of new Christian churches as the religion spread throughout the Roman Empire
Syncretism between Roman and early Christian architectural styles.
The Roman conquest of Judea, the birthplace of the Christian religion

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>"The Abbot who is worthy to be over a monastery, ought always to be mindful of what he is called, and make his works square with his name of Superior [...] Therefore, the Abbot should never teach, prescribe, or command (which God forbid) anything contrary to the laws of the Lord; but his commands and teaching should be instilled like a leaven of divine justice into the minds of his disciples.”


From: _The Holy Rule of St. Benedict_, c. 480-550 CE

**The monastic practice described in the passage was most similar to that of which of the following religions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Buddhism
Judaism
Sikhism
Confucianism

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>"The Abbot who is worthy to be over a monastery, ought always to be mindful of what he is called, and make his works square with his name of Superior. For he is believed to hold the place of Christ in the monastery, when he is called by his name, according to the saying of the Apostle 'You have received the spirit of adoption of sons, whereby we cry Abba (Father)'. Therefore, the Abbot should never teach, prescribe, or command (which God forbid) anything contrary to the laws of the Lord; but his commands and teaching should be instilled like a leaven of divine justice into the minds of his disciples.”

From: _The Holy Rule of St. Benedict_, c. 480-550 CE

**Ideas similar to those expressed in the passage have directly contributed to the development of which of the following aspects of early Christian history?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increase in monastic orders
Codification of scriptures
Increased gender hierarchy in the Church
Spread of Christianity to Rome

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**This painting is best seen as evidence for which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Moses striking the rock in the desert, a prototype of baptism. Painting on the wall of an early Christian catacomb, before 3rd century CE.
options.caption: Moses striking the rock in the desert, a prototype of baptism. Painting on the wall of an early Christian catacomb, before 3rd century CE. Image credit: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_Christian_art_and_architecture#/media/File:Moses_striking_the_rock_in_the_desert.jpg)

choices.content: Jewish influence on Christianity
Christian influence on Judaism
Hellenic influence on Christianity
Jewish influence on Hellenism

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage and answer the question. 

> Notes: Arians were a Christian sect the Church considered heretical; Milan was the capital of the Western Roman Empire in 386 CE.

>"In 386 CE the emperor gave the Arians the right of assembly and declared that violators would be . . . liable to the death penalty. When Ambrose, bishop of Milan, again refused to allow the Arians the use of church property, the emperor issued a decree exiling him. Ambrose replied that the emperor was within the church, not over it, and that in matters of faith the layman has no jurisdiction over the priest. The sympathies of the people were again with him, and the emperor did not attempt to enforce the decree of exile." 

>From William K. Boyd, _The Ecclesiastical Edicts of the Theodosian Code_ (1905).

**The information in this passage could best be used as evidence for which of the following claims about the fourth century CE?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Christian leaders took over some of the authority that had traditionally belonged to Roman emperors
Roman emperors had complete control over Christian leaders and could exile them for disagreement
Church leaders and Roman emperors agreed on what Christian beliefs and behaviors were considered orthodox
The common people were unwilling to support Church leaders who contradicted the emperor's orders

assessment_items.question_content:
Study the map below and answer the question that follows:

[[☃ image 2]]


**Which of the following changes in the Roman Empire best explains the increased spread of Christianity after c. 300 CE shown in this map?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The spread of Christianity was **not** equal in both rural and urban areas.

options.alt: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 to 800 CE. Spain, France, Italy, Greece, Asia Minor, North Africa, Germany and England were all Christianized by 800 CE.
options.caption: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 CE to 800 CE. Dark Blue shows spread up to 300 CE; Green shows spread between 300 CE and 600 CE; Yellow shows spread after 600 CE.  Image credit: [Mapmania](https://www.mapmania.org/map/60609/diffusion_of_christianity)

choices.content: Emperor Constantine made it legal to practice Christianity with the Edict of Milan
Rome removed connections between the state's political power and religious practices
The fall of the Roman Empire led to the end of persecution of Christians
Christian Germanic tribes migrated into the empire and forced Romans to convert

assessment_items.question_content:
Study this map and answer the question that follows.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following claims best explains the pattern of Christianity's spread shown in this map?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Christianity didn't spread less between 300 and 600 CE than it did during the previous 300 years.

options.alt: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 to 800 CE. Spain, France, Italy, Greece, Asia Minor, North Africa, Germany and England were all Christianized by 800 CE.
options.caption: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 CE to 800 CE. Dark Blue shows spread up to 300 CE; Green shows spread between 300 CE and 600 CE; Yellow shows spread after 600 CE.  Image credit:  [Mapmania](https://www.mapmania.org/map/60609/diffusion_of_christianity)

choices.content: Christianity's spread prior to the ninth century was aided by its status as the official religion of the Roman Empire
Christianity spread the most between 600 CE and 800 CE because it was more popular beyond the old boundaries of the Roman Empire
Christianity spread through Europe mostly after 600 CE because the fall of the Roman Empire ended persecution of Christians
Christianity remained a predominantly urban religion even after the fall of the Roman Empire led to reduced urbanization
